terms.definition: Asymptotic upper bound on a function's growth; O(f(n)) means the algorithm's cost grows no faster than f(n) up to constants.
Asymptotic lower bound on growth; Ω(f(n)) means the algorithm's cost grows no slower than f(n).
Tight asymptotic bound; Θ(f(n)) means both O(f(n)) and Ω(f(n)) — exact growth class.
Behavior of an algorithm as input size approaches infinity, ignoring constant factors and lower-order terms.
Averaging the cost of an operation across a long sequence; an occasional O(n) step can still amortize to O(1) per call.
The maximum running time over all possible inputs of size n; the strongest performance guarantee.
Expected running time under an assumed input distribution; usually less pessimistic than worst-case.
The minimum running time on the most favorable input of size n; rarely useful by itself but explains adaptive algorithms.
Big-O of the number of basic operations as a function of n.
Big-O of the extra memory used as a function of n, beyond the input itself.
